title: Keisner Dominates Jeddah Sprint as Last‑Lap Crash Eliminates Roy and Kevu
meta: Published: 25 April 2026 | By Car Fok, Conventional Race Chronicler & Ex‑Ferrari Driver | F1CC News
caption: Robin Keisner took a commanding sprint victory in Jeddah, but the race was overshadowed by a violent final‑lap crash.
body:
Robin Keisner took a dominant victory in the Saudi Arabian Grand Prix sprint race, leading from pole position to the chequered flag as the Audi driver finished 8.3 seconds clear of Mercedes' Maikel Zwart. The result was Keisner's second sprint win of the season, following his Shanghai success, but the race was overshadowed by a final‑lap collision between Jason Roy and Quarter Kevu that eliminated both drivers and brought a chaotic conclusion to an eventful 17‑lap contest.

Keisner's victory was built on a flawless launch and a pace advantage that no rival could match. By the time the race reached its midway point, the German had built a lead of over three seconds, and he managed the gap through two restarts without ever looking threatened. Behind him, the order was scrambled by strategic gambles, a Virtual Safety Car, and the kind of late‑race desperation that Jeddah's concrete walls seem to invite.

The sprint began under punishing conditions — 30 degrees ambient, 43 degrees on track — with Keisner converting pole into an immediate lead. Jason Roy, starting from the front row, held second, while Eetu Väisänen slotted into third. A strategic split emerged early: a group of drivers including Roy, Philippe Verhaeren, Riley Swansson, and Sarah Springfield had opted for medium compound tyres, gambling that the soft runners would fade and allow them to charge in the closing laps. Everyone else, including Keisner, was on softs.

The race's first major incident came on lap 6. Philippe Verhaeren, running strongly in the top six, lost control on his own at Turn 22 and hit the wall. The Red Bull driver was unhurt, but the car was heavily damaged — a wheel torn from the chassis. The Virtual Safety Car was deployed, bunching the field and giving the soft runners an unexpected reprieve from degradation. At the restart, the race ignited. Roy locked up under braking and lost momentum, allowing Väisänen and Zwart to sweep past. Zwart's pace on the soft tyre was eye‑catching; he climbed to second with a series of decisive moves, his Mercedes looking more competitive than at any point since Australia.

As the laps ticked down, the medium runners began to close. Roy, recovering from his lock‑up, found rhythm and started carving back through the field. Swansson passed Zhao Lei for eighth. The top eight compressed into a DRS train, every position fiercely contested. Richard Hicks, the second Audi, produced a series of audacious overtakes to climb to fourth, briefly challenging Zwart for third before the Mercedes driver repassed him. The racing was frantic but clean — until the final lap.

Into the last sector, with positions still unsettled, Jason Roy attempted a move on Quarter Kevu. The two made contact, and both cars were launched into the barriers. The impact was heavy, and while both drivers emerged without injury, the damage to the cars was extensive. Roy and Kevu were classified as non‑finishers, their promising afternoons ending in the wall. The final order was taken from the running order at the moment of the incident, with Keisner already across the line and the remaining positions frozen behind the crash site.

Zwart's second place is his best sprint result of the season and a timely boost for a Mercedes team that has struggled for consistent form. Väisänen's third adds six points to his championship lead, while Richard Hicks and Riley Swansson both delivered strong drives to complete the top five — Hicks continuing Audi's excellent weekend, Swansson scoring valuable points on his second Ferrari appearance. Zhao Lei took sixth for McLaren, with Michiil MaraDöner seventh, and Sarah Springfield collecting the final point in eighth.

Further back, Jack Tef finished ninth after starting thirteenth, the Williams team principal narrowly missing out on points. Lukas Wright completed the top ten for Racing Bulls. Eren Aygen endured a difficult afternoon, finishing seventeenth after being eliminated in SQ1 — a weekend to forget for the championship contender. Afonso Cachinho, in the Haas, finished eleventh, while Joshua Nathanial could only manage thirteenth after also failing to escape SQ1.

The stewards confirmed they would investigate the Roy‑Kevu collision after the race. The result strengthens Keisner's hold on third in the championship, while Väisänen's podium extends his lead at the top. For Roy, the DNF is a blow to a season that has promised much but delivered inconsistently. The paddock now turns its attention to qualifying for the main grand prix, where Roy's participation may depend on the extent of the damage to his Red Bull. Jeddah, as always, has left its mark.
